perturbation.original_text: The Recipient undertakes not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose except the Purpose, without first obtaining the written agreement of the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will endeavor not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose except the Purpose, without first attempting to obtain the written agreement of the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'undertakes not to' to 'will endeavor not to' weakens the obligation. Replacing 'obtaining' with 'attempting to obtain' further introduces ambiguity as it doesn't guarantee the agreement is actually obtained.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Confidence - Legal obligation of confidence
perturbation.law_citation: Various case laws, such as Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd [1969] RPC 41
perturbation.law_explanation: English law, while not codified in the same way as statutes, has a strong basis in case law for breach of confidence. The modification weakens the confidentiality obligation, making it harder to prove a breach because 'endeavor' and 'attempting' suggest an effort that might not be sufficient to uphold the duty of confidence established in common law. Case law such as Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd establishes clear criteria for breach of confidence, and this modification undermines those criteria.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: property | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you property Property is anything (items or attributes/tangible or intangible) that can be owned by a person or entity . Property is the most complete right to something; the owner can possess, use, transfer or dispose of it. According to California Civil Code (CIV) Section 657 and 663 , the property is divided into real property and personal property. Real property consists of land and items which are immovable by law (like structures or bridges); property which is not real property is personal property. Property that exists in physical form is tangible property; like houses, apples, and cellphones. Property which cannot be physically held is considered intangible property; like copyrights, trademarks, or the goodwill of a company. Copyrights , trademarks , trade secrets , and patents are also intellectual property . If the property is held by the government or a public department, it should be listed as public property ; otherwise it would be considered private property . [Last reviewed in March of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords property Wex COMMERCE commercial activities PROPERTY property & real estate law trusts inheritances & estates business law business organizations wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions intellectual property copyright law patent law trade secrets law trademark law legal education and practice property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The change from 'undertakes not to' to 'will endeavor not to' weakens the obligation, making it potentially unenforceable. Additionally, changing 'obtaining' to 'attempting to obtain' introduces further ambiguity about whether agreement is secured. This undermines the original intent of strict confidentiality. While no specific statute is cited in the scraped snippets, the 'Law Explanation' references the common law duty of confidence and case law such as Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd. The modifications introduce ambiguity that could make it difficult to prove a breach of confidence, thus creating a legal contradiction as per the provided definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information secure and not to disclose it to any third party [except to its employees [and professional advi sers] who need to know the same for the Purpose, who know they owe a duty of confidence to the Discloser and who are bound by obligations equivalent to those in clause 2 above and this clause 3.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will try to keep the Confidential Information reasonably secure and avoid disclosing it to any third party, as far as practicable.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'undertakes to keep secure' with 'will try to keep reasonably secure' and 'not to disclose' with 'avoid disclosing...as far as practicable' significantly reduces the level of responsibility and introduces subjectivity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Confidence - Legal obligation of confidence
perturbation.law_citation: Various case laws, such as Coco v A N Clark (Engineers) Ltd [1969] RPC 41
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification undermines the strict duty of confidence by introducing vague terms like 'reasonably secure' and 'as far as practicable.' This contradicts established case law that demands a clear and demonstrable effort to maintain confidentiality, not merely an attempt to do so within the bounds of practical convenience.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: intellectual property | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you intellectual property Intellectual property (I.P. or IP) is a type of property encompassing the products of original human thought. Common examples of intellectual property include: the contents of a book, designs of an invention, computer software, company logos, and music. Intellectual property differs from other forms of property in that it is non-tangible, and non-rivalrous. Unlike other forms of property, intellectual property can be used by infinitely many people without depriving the original owner of the use of their property. This characteristic of intellectual property makes it particularly vulnerable to free rider problems, creating unique challenges for authors and inventors who wish to monetize their work. Intellectual property rights were created to ensure that authors and inventors are compensated for their efforts to incentivize the production of further works for the benefit of the public. They combat the free rider problem by giving intellectual property owners a near monopoly over the use of their creations for a set period of time. This allows intellectual property owners to license their intellectual property, and to recover monetarily from those who infringe on their intellectual property rights through piracy or wrongful use. However, intellectual property rights are not absolute, and are subject to a myriad of exceptions and affirmative defenses , such as the fair use doctrine. Within the broad category of intellectual property, there are four subcategories of intellectual property: patent , copyright , trademark , and trade secrets . In the United States, patents and copyright are regulated exclusively by federal law, as outlined in the Intellectual Property Clause . Trademarks are areas of shared jurisdiction between federal and state governments, with the federal government deriving their power to regulate trademarks through the Commerce Clause . Trade secrets are largely regulated by states through unfair competition laws. [Last reviewed in June of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords property PROPERTY RIGHTS intellectual property U.S. CONSTITUTION constitutional law Wex CIVICS the Constitution COMMERCE commercial activities PROPERTY business law wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions constitutional law intellectual property copyright law patent law trade secrets law trademark law property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text creates a strict obligation to maintain confidentiality, whereas the changed text introduces a much weaker standard of 'trying' to keep information 'reasonably' secure and 'avoiding' disclosure 'as far as practicable'. The Law Explanation states this change 'undermines the strict duty of confidence'. This is an Ambiguities Legal Contradiction. The original text contained unambiguous legal obligations. The modified text replaces these obligations with ambiguous ones, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined by the Perturbation Definitions.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will, on request from the Discloser, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information to the Discloser and will not retain any copies or records of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will, if feasible and upon reasonable request from the Discloser, attempt to return most copies and records of the Confidential Information that are readily available to the Discloser, and will try not to retain too many copies or records of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing 'if feasible and upon reasonable request,' 'attempt to return most,' 'readily available,' and 'try not to retain too many' all add layers of ambiguity, making the obligation far less definitive.
perturbation.law_citation: Attorney-General v Guardian Newspapers Ltd (No 2) [1990] 1 AC 109
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification diminishes the recipient's clear obligation to return all confidential information. Case law requires a clear and unambiguous handling and returning of confidential information, which the perturbed text does not fulfill.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces significant ambiguities, weakening the recipient's obligation to return confidential information. The original text mandates the return of 'all copies and records,' while the changed text introduces 'if feasible,' 'attempt to return most,' 'readily available,' and 'try not to retain too many,' creating loopholes. The law explanation supports that case law requires a clear and unambiguous handling of confidential information, which the perturbed text fails to provide. Because the changes make the obligation much less definitive, it creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The undertakings in clauses 2 and 3 will continue in force [indefinitely.] [for [insert number] years from the date of this Agreement.]
perturbation.changed_text: The undertakings in clauses 2 and 3 will likely continue in force for a period deemed reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the definite timeframe with 'likely continue in force for a period deemed reasonable' introduces significant ambiguity about the duration of the confidentiality obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Limitation Act 1980 sets specific time limits for bringing legal actions, including those related to contract breaches. By making the duration of the NDA 'a period deemed reasonable,' the modification conflicts with the legal certainty required by the Limitation Act. The statute demands a defined period for which obligations are enforceable. The perturbed clause would likely be unenforceable due to its vagueness, undermining the legal basis for pursuing a breach of confidence claim after a certain point, contrary to statutory limitations.

perturbation.justification: The original text provides a definite timeframe, while the changed text introduces ambiguity regarding the duration of the obligations. The Law Explanation states that this conflicts with the legal certainty required, because it undermines the legal basis for pursuing a breach of confidence claim after a certain point. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Limitation Act 1980, which implies the existence of a defined period for legal actions related to contract breaches, and that this Act sets specific time limits. The changed text replaces a definite timeframe with 'a period deemed reasonable,' creating an Ambiguous Legal Obligation, which is contradictory.
